pair_convention.spot_explanation: USD/CHF at 0.7797 means 1 USD buys 0.7797 Swiss Francs. A rising pair means the USD is strengthening against the CHF (more CHF per USD). A falling pair means the CHF is strengthening against the USD (fewer CHF per USD). In the current context, USD/CHF has fallen approximately 11.6% over the past year, reflecting sustained CHF appreciation driven by safe-haven demand, Switzerland's large current account surplus, and structural USD deterioration.
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (USD weakens vs CHF, CHF appreciates)

executive_summary: USD/CHF has fallen approximately 11.6% over the past year, with the pair trading near 0.7797 — within striking distance of its 52-week low of 0.7604 and well below the 200-day SMA of 0.7937. The pair is defined by a ferocious bilateral collision: the US dollar is fighting the world's most structurally impaired fiscal trajectory against the world's most overvalued-yet-structurally-defended safe-haven currency. The Iran war that began February 28 has placed both currencies in their respective safe-haven modes simultaneously — USD drawing flight-to-quality flows as the reserve currency and net energy exporter, CHF drawing flows as the premier safe-haven currency of choice — creating a tug-of-war that has compressed the pair into an unusually tight range between 0.778 and 0.785. The dominant bilateral differential is the -363bps policy rate gap (Fed at 3.625% vs SNB at 0.00%), which pays carry to USD holders, but this advantage is increasingly offset by structural USD deterioration — stagflationary data, twin deficits at 8.8% of GDP, declining COFER share — while CHF is buttressed by Switzerland's 7% current account surplus, AAA sovereign rating, and record gold export revenues. The bilateral carry-to-vol ratio of +0.38 is the highest in USD's G10 peer set, yet USD/CHF has still fallen 11.6% over 12 months as structural CHF appreciation completely overwhelmed the carry advantage. Our bilateral stance is Bearish USD/CHF tactically (pair falls, CHF strengthens) at Conviction 2/5, upgrading to Bearish USD/CHF strategically at Conviction 3/5 as the Fed-SNB divergence and external balance differentials compound over six months. The March 19 SNB meeting following the March 18 FOMC is the near-term inflection catalyst that will resolve the key near-term uncertainty.

market_context.valuation_assessment: USD/CHF at 0.7797 sits approximately 22% below the OECD PPP fair value of approximately 1.00, meaning USD is deeply cheap versus PPP and CHF is deeply expensive. The CHF dossier's PPP table shows a 28% deviation with USD, with implied fair value of 1.00. However, CHF's structural PPP overvaluation has persisted for over two decades and is non-actionable as a tactical signal. The USD dossier identifies a -33% PPP deviation versus CHF, confirming USD is the most undervalued against CHF in its G10 peer group. At BIS REER levels — USD at 106.9 (62nd percentile, moderately expensive) and CHF at approximately 103.6 (72nd percentile, expensive) — the bilateral REER argues for modest pair upside but not dramatically so. The current spot level reflects the war premium on both safe-haven legs partially offsetting each other, leaving the pair modestly below its rate-differential-implied fair value.

market_context.recent_regime: USD/CHF has been in a sustained downtrend for over a year, declining from approximately 0.93 in early 2025 to 0.76 at the January 27, 2026 low before partially recovering to 0.78–0.79. Over 2025, the franc gained almost 13% against the US dollar, extending those gains into 2026 and hitting an 11-year high against the greenback. The Iran war (February 28) briefly created a counter-trend bounce — USD's safe-haven bid lifted USD/CHF from near 0.76 toward 0.780–0.785 — but CHF's even stronger safe-haven character capped the bounce. The regime is now a dual-safe-haven tug-of-war: USD draws flight-to-quality flows from global reserve demand and energy exporter status, while CHF draws structural safe-haven flows from its AAA credentials and gold correlation. Both the US Dollar and Swiss Franc remain the only reliable safe havens in the current energy-driven risk-off environment, creating muted pair directional signals relative to other CHF crosses.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The bilateral carry for USD/CHF is +363bps annualised in USD's favour (Fed at 3.625% vs SNB at 0.00%), the largest positive carry among all USD crosses after adjusting for CHF's zero rate floor. The carry-to-vol ratio of +0.38 is the highest across all USD pairs and one of the highest in G10. Despite this structural advantage, carry is emphatically NOT working — USD/CHF has fallen 11.6% over 12 months as structural CHF appreciation has completely overwhelmed the carry advantage. The safe-haven bid in the current Iran war regime fully negates carry logic. Academic research confirms that safe-haven currencies depreciate against other currencies in normal times due to carry trades, but in times of stress a flight to quality occurs causing carry trade positions to unwind, and safe-haven currencies appreciate. The current Iran war regime is a textbook carry-destruction environment for USD/CHF. The forward market embeds the +363bps rate differential in USD's favour — forward points are positive (USD/CHF forwards above spot at each tenor from 1M to 6M) — but our expected spot at each tenor is materially below the forward-implied rate, creating a negative edge for USD longs and a positive structural alpha opportunity for CHF longs willing to accept the carry drag.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The Fed-SNB differential of +363bps is the single largest bilateral carry differential in USD/CHF's history and mechanically favours USD via the interest rate parity channel. The USD dossier notes the UST-Swiss 10Y spread at +390bps, the widest in G10. However, the tactical question is not the level of the differential but its impact on pair direction during the current risk-off regime. The SNB is firmly pinned at 0.00%, with the March 19 meeting expected to deliver a hold (approximately 85% probability) and communication focused on FX intervention willingness rather than rate changes. The Fed is also holding on March 18 (95%+ probability). The near-term tactical signal is therefore dominated by what each central bank communicates rather than what they do. A hawkish Fed dot plot (zero 2026 cuts) would be pair-bullish through the rate channel; a dovish SNB with specific intervention thresholds disclosed could support a pair recovery. UBS projects that USD/CHF should stabilize around 0.78 over 2026, noting that the large rate differential provides a structural anchor for the pair. However, with the carry failing to prevent CHF appreciation and both central banks holding, the pair direction is determined by factors other than rates at the tactical horizon. The Swiss 2Y yield at -0.13% confirms markets are partially pricing negative SNB rates, a scenario that historically has a mixed record for CHF direction — initial CHF weakness on announcement, but structural CHF strength persists as deflation expectations reinforce safe-haven demand.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the carry advantage entirely into the forward curve (positive forward points for USD/CHF). It does NOT price structural USD deterioration that would erode this differential — specifically, the market underprices the speed at which the Fed could deliver cuts if NFP continues negative, which would compress the differential by 50-100bps within 6 months.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic trajectory of the Fed-SNB differential is diverging against USD/CHF (bearish pair). The Fed's December dot plot projects the funds rate at 3.4% by year-end 2026 — one cut from 3.625%. If the labour market continues to deteriorate (NFP negative in 3 of 5 months), the Fed could deliver two cuts (50bps reduction), compressing the differential from +363bps toward +313bps. The SNB, in contrast, has minimal room to cut further (already at 0.00%) and would only cut to -0.25% under a severe deflation scenario — which would paradoxically be CHF-negative tactically but reinforce safe-haven flows strategically. The critical dynamic is the rate path asymmetry: the Fed has significant downside rate risk (multiple cuts possible), while the SNB has minimal room to diverge. This asymmetry means the bilateral differential is structurally set to compress over 6 months. UBS believes regional macroeconomic factors will regain prominence in 2026, with interest rate differentials still supporting CHF, but at a somewhat diminished pace as the SNB's rate ceiling at 0.00% limits its response function. The SNB's preferred tool — FX intervention — creates short-term resistance but does not change the strategic trajectory.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The growth-inflation differential between the US and Switzerland is the starkest in G10. The US presents a stagflationary mix — NFP at -92K alongside core PCE at 3.0% — that simultaneously prevents Fed easing (inflation too high) and demands it (growth too weak). Switzerland presents the opposite: robust fiscal health, near-zero inflation (0.1% YoY), and modest but positive GDP growth (+0.2% QoQ in Q4). The US/Swiss inflation differential of +2.3pp (2.5% US core CPI vs 0.2–0.4% Swiss core) means that holding USD delivers approximately 2.3% less real purchasing power preservation annually than holding CHF — a profound structural advantage for CHF that the options market is beginning to price more aggressively. The US data surprise differential is decisively negative: February NFP of -92K vs consensus +80K was a 172K miss; US Composite PMI at 51.9 has converged to the Eurozone. Switzerland's KOF barometer at 104.2 is at its highest since September 2024. The asymmetric sensitivity of USD/CHF to each economy's data: US CPI and NFP release move the pair materially (50–80 pips), while Swiss CPI is a secondary driver unless it goes negative. The February CPI release is the immediate binary catalyst.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic growth differential is shifting against USD/CHF. US GDP consensus for 2026 is deteriorating (2.3% SEP projection likely being revised lower), while Swiss GDP consensus clusters around 1.0–1.1% — a closing gap that removes the US exceptionalism premium. The critical transmission for USD/CHF: if US labour market deterioration accelerates (unemployment toward 4.8–5.0%), the Fed will cut more aggressively than priced, widening the real carry advantage for CHF (lower nominal rate but lower inflation = higher real rate). Switzerland's oil shock pass-through is minimal — energy accounts for approximately 5% of Swiss CPI — meaning the Iran war's inflationary impact primarily hits the US, reinforcing the stagflationary narrative for USD and amplifying the differential. Swiss manufacturing contraction (PMI at 47.4 for 38 months) is a structural headwind, but the services sector and pharmaceutical exports provide offsetting support.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: This is the most distinctive bilateral dynamic for USD/CHF versus any other pair: both currencies are safe havens, creating a dampening effect on risk-off pair moves that doesn't exist in, say, AUD/JPY or NZD/CHF. When VIX spikes, USD/CHF does not have a consistent directional bias because both legs benefit simultaneously. Current analysis suggests both the US Dollar and Swiss Franc remain the only reliable safe havens in the energy-driven risk-off environment. The Iran war has validated this: USD/CHF has barely moved (-1.2% over the past month) despite VIX spiking from 17 to 35.3, precisely because the safe-haven bids partially cancel each other. The tiebreaker in the current regime: CHF has outperformed USD in safe-haven terms because the Swiss franc has solidified itself as the premier safe-haven currency of choice, while USD's safe-haven credentials are being eroded by fiscal concerns, political pressure on the Fed, and the April 2025 precedent where USD sold off during equity weakness when the US itself was the source of instability. USD is still a safe haven, but not always the first responder — in 2025 and into 2026, the yen and franc often take the lead.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the safe-haven competition between USD and CHF resolves in CHF's favour as the USD's structural safe-haven deterioration accelerates. Academic research shows that unlike classic safe-haven currencies such as the Japanese yen and Swiss franc, the dollar appreciates only temporarily during ordinary risk-off events — during such events, the dollar weakens persistently against the yen and Swiss franc. This academic finding has clear bilateral implications: once the war's acute phase passes (historical median: 6–8 weeks for geopolitical safe-haven premiums), USD reverts to structural weakness while CHF retains its safe-haven credentials due to Switzerland's net creditor position and fiscal strength. Julius Baer noted that erratic trade policies compound with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act placing the US on an unsustainable debt trajectory, while Trump's pressure on US Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell also undermined investors' confidence in the dollar. CHF's safe-haven advantage is reinforced by Switzerland's net foreign asset position (positive, per Habib and Stracca 2012 criteria), while the US has a substantially negative NFA position — a structural erosion of USD's safe-haven claim.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The positioning picture for USD/CHF is genuinely balanced. On the USD leg, aggregate net speculative shorts at -$19.6B (~85th percentile bearish) represent crowded short-USD positioning that creates squeeze risk if war escalates. On the CHF leg, leveraged funds at +4,456 contracts net long are building but nowhere near extremes (+30K would be notable). Asset managers hold a massive -54,527 contracts net short CHF (structural carry positioning) representing potential short-covering fuel. The SNB's active FX intervention — sight deposits rose CHF 4.4bn in the first post-war week — is the unique bilateral flow that doesn't exist on the USD side. The FXF ETF has attracted +$147.68M in 3-month inflows, confirming sustained retail/institutional demand for CHF exposure. The net pair positioning implication: USD shorts are large and crowded (USD/CHF upside squeeze risk), while CHF longs are moderate and building (USD/CHF downside pressure). These forces are approximately balanced, producing a neutral tactical stance.

The probability of all three converging is approximately 15–20%, but any single one could generate a 200–400 pip USD/CHF rally that creates an attractive re-entry point for strategic CHF longs.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The net commodity and external balance differentials decisively favour CHF over USD at the tactical horizon. For the oil shock impact: USD benefits modestly as US is a net energy exporter with WTI at $83–91 providing terms of trade benefit; Switzerland imports approximately 95% of energy needs but energy is only approximately 5% of CPI, so the negative impact is mild. Net pair impact of oil at $83–91 is mildly USD/CHF positive, but overwhelmed by other factors. For gold: USD/CHF gold impact is mildly negative (gold competes with Treasuries as safe haven alternative), while CHF is strongly positive as Switzerland's refineries process approximately 25% of world's gold — gold at $5,200+ dramatically boosts Swiss trade surplus and CHF export revenues. Net pair impact of gold at $5,200+: strongly CHF positive (pair falls materially). The current account differential is decisive: US at -3.6% of GDP versus Switzerland at +7.0% of GDP — a 10.6pp bilateral gap that is one of the widest in G10, generating structural CHF demand of approximately CHF 30–35bn over 6 months against a US structural deficit requiring $3.6T of annual foreign capital inflows.

The cumulative US-Swiss bilateral current account divergence — US deficit of approximately $150B over 6 months versus Swiss surplus of CHF 30–35bn — represents a structural pair-bearish flow. The gold amplification channel is the novel bilateral element: at UBS's 6-month target of $6,200/oz for gold, Swiss gold export revenues could expand by an additional CHF 2–3bn, further widening the bilateral surplus advantage. The US twin deficit at 8.8% of GDP (fiscal 5.9% + CA 2.9%) contrasts sharply with Switzerland's near-zero structural deficit. Declining foreign UST demand (Japan, China reducing holdings) reduces the financing of the US CA deficit, creating structural USD/CHF downward pressure. The US-Swiss bilateral tariff situation (reduced from 39% to 15% but with a March 31 deadline for a legally binding agreement) adds event risk around the external balance.

strategic.what_is_priced: External balance is partially priced through the structural DXY decline. The underpriced element: gold at $5,200+ generating record Swiss export revenues is a genuinely novel bilateral dynamic not present in prior CHF appreciation cycles.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: USD is net energy exporter — WTI at $83–91 provides modest terms of trade benefit (oil up = mild USD positive). Gold at $5,200+ is mildly USD-negative as it competes with Treasuries as a safe haven alternative, reducing net Treasury demand.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: CHF is strongly positively correlated with gold via Switzerland's role as processor of approximately 25% of world's gold — gold at $5,200+ generates unprecedented Swiss gold export revenues (~25% of total Swiss exports by value), creating structural CHF demand. Switzerland imports approximately 95% of energy but energy is only approximately 5% of CPI, limiting oil shock pass-through.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil impact netting: US net energy exporter (oil up = mild USD positive, +); Switzerland net energy importer but energy only 5% of CPI (mild negative, −). Net pair effect: mildly USD/CHF positive (pair rises slightly). Step 2 — Gold impact netting: USD mildly negative from gold (safe haven competition, −); CHF strongly positive from gold (Swiss gold export revenue windfall at $5,200+/oz, ++). Net pair effect: strongly CHF positive (pair falls materially). Step 3 — Current account netting: US deficit at -3.6% GDP (structural USD-negative, −−); Swiss surplus at +7.0% GDP (structural CHF-positive, ++). Net pair effect: strongly CHF positive (pair falls). Step 4 — Aggregate: Gold and current account channels both strongly CHF positive, overwhelming the modest oil channel USD positive. Net conclusion: external balance and commodity channels are decisively USD/CHF bearish.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% (WTI from ~$87 to ~$96): Net USD/CHF modestly positive (+50–80 pips) as US energy exporter terms of trade benefit outweighs Switzerland's modest energy import cost increase. However, the oil up scenario simultaneously pushes gold higher (energy/commodity correlated), generating an offsetting CHF positive that reduces the net pair impact to approximately +30–50 pips.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% (WTI from ~$87 to ~$78): Net USD/CHF mildly negative (−30–50 pips) as US energy exporter revenues compress while Switzerland's import costs fall. The oil down scenario coincides with de-escalation (war resolution), which would collapse the CHF safe-haven premium more powerfully than the trade terms effect — the safe-haven unwind would dominate, driving USD/CHF up 2–4% and overwhelming the minor oil terms of trade effect.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Gold is the dominant commodity driver for USD/CHF. Switzerland's processing of approximately 25% of world gold means gold at $5,200+ generates a structural CHF demand channel of unprecedented scale — each $100/oz gold increase adds approximately CHF 250–300M to monthly Swiss exports. This gold-CHF amplifier is a novel bilateral dynamic absent from prior USD/CHF downtrend cycles and not fully priced by the market, making it the most important commodity variable to monitor.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The guidance contrast between the Fed and SNB creates an unusual bilateral dynamic: the Fed is explicitly data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting (high policy uncertainty), while the SNB has provided the most unambiguous communication since 2015 — it will intervene in FX markets if CHF appreciation continues. These opposing communication styles largely offset each other tactically. The Fed's March 18 dot plot is the critical near-term guidance event: zero cuts (hawkish) would be pair-bullish (+1–2% USD/CHF), one cut (neutral) is priced, two cuts (dovish) would be pair-bearish. The SNB's March 19 communication will be scrutinised for: (1) revised conditional inflation forecast — if it drops below 0.2% for 2026, cut risk rises; (2) intervention thresholds — any specific EUR/CHF or USD/CHF level disclosed would be a tactical landmark; (3) negative rate readiness — any softening of the hurdle is high language would be CHF-negative. Both central banks have similar near-term guidance uncertainty (high), producing a neutral net bilateral signal.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic guidance differential tilts modestly bearish for USD/CHF. The Fed Chair transition (Powell departing May 15, Warsh to be confirmed) creates institutional guidance uncertainty that is USD-negative — markets cannot anchor on a predictable Fed reaction function during the transition. The SNB, despite having only four meetings per year, has a clear and credible communication framework: it intervenes in FX, it will use negative rates if necessary, and it does not pre-commit to specific levels (learning from 2015's floor removal). The SNB's credibility on intervention is actually enhanced by its willingness to suffer large losses (CHF 53.1bn in exchange rate losses in 2025) — the market knows the SNB will act. The contrast: a Fed in transition under political pressure versus an SNB with a clear, activated mandate creates a guidance credibility gap that marginally benefits CHF.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The June 14 Swiss referendum on No to 10-Million Switzerland is the most significant underpriced bilateral event risk for USD/CHF over the strategic horizon. Passage (current polls: ~48% in favour) would trigger the guillotine clause, terminating all bilateral agreements with the EU — a scenario the CHF dossier identifies as potentially CHF-negative (3–5% EUR/CHF depreciation). For USD/CHF, the relationship is complex: initial capital repatriation flows could be CHF-positive (Swiss investors selling foreign assets), followed by structural negative impact from lost EU market access (CHF-negative). The July 24 Section 122 tariff expiry (10% flat rate replacing IEEPA) adds bilateral tariff uncertainty that affects Swiss exporters directly. The Fed Chair transition in May is a USD-specific negative event.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The US-Swiss fiscal contrast is the most extreme in G10 for this specific pair. The US lost its last AAA rating (Moody's, May 2025), carrying AA+/Aa1/AA+; Switzerland retains AAA from all five agencies with federal debt at approximately 16% of GDP versus US at approximately 125% of GDP. This is not a driver of near-term tactical moves but creates a structural gravitational pull toward CHF that activates during risk-off events — exactly the current environment. The US net interest expense at $970B (~19% of federal revenue) represents a compounding fiscal challenge, while Switzerland runs a CHF +0.3bn fiscal surplus. The spread in 10Y CDS — US near the highest in G10 for an AAA-adjacent sovereign, Switzerland near zero — quantifies the safe-haven premium. Tactically, fiscal risk is background but not inactive: continued Iran war spending adds to the US deficit without a corresponding Swiss impact.

Position sizing should account for the fat-tail profile: CHF's 2015 floor removal caused a 30% intraday move in EUR/CHF, and USD/CHF has exhibited similar extreme tail behaviour. For strategic positions, execution in the spot market with staged entry (1/3 on initial entry, 1/3 on breach of 0.7700, 1/3 on breach of 0.7604) is preferred over a single large fill.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: For USD/CHF specifically, USD liquidity conditions have a direct pair impact through the cross-currency basis. The USD-CHF 3M cross-currency basis is estimated at approximately -10 to -20bps (CHF borrows at a discount to LIBOR/SOFR via USD), meaning it is modestly cheap to fund CHF-denominated assets by borrowing in USD — a marginal CHF-positive. The Fed's QT-to-RMP pivot (injecting approximately $40B/month in T-bills) is increasing dollar supply, which structurally narrows cross-currency bases globally. SNB USD swap line usage at $89M is essentially zero, confirming there is no acute offshore CHF funding stress in the USD market. SOFR at 3.65% is stable. The tactical dynamic: the war has increased global USD funding demand (commodity hedges, margin calls), which could widen the cross-currency basis temporarily — a USD-positive signal that offsets some of the structural USD weakness. Cross-currency basis is typically quoted as a negative figure; when it is more negative, it reflects a greater relative shortage of a currency. A widening USD-CHF basis (more negative) would signal dollar shortage, temporarily supporting USD/CHF.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic USD liquidity trajectory is bearish for USD/CHF. The Fed's pivot from QT to RMPs ($40B/month × 6 months = approximately $240B injection) reduces the scarcity premium for USD, narrowing cross-currency bases and reducing hedging costs for foreign (including Swiss) investors in USD assets. This paradoxically reduces demand for spot USD — foreign investors need less USD when hedging costs fall. The US dollar's exorbitant privilege — the structural demand created by its reserve currency status — is being eroded by reserve diversification (COFER USD share 56.92%, 30-year low) while Swiss franc demand is increasing (CHF reserve share quadrupled to 0.8%). The dollar smile framework places USD moving from the left side (war: crisis safe-haven) toward the bottom (ample liquidity, risk-on) over 6 months — both scenarios are USD-negative for USD/CHF. Research confirms the dollar appreciates only temporarily during ordinary risk-off events, and during such events it weakens persistently against the Swiss franc.

tactical.rationale: The tactical picture for USD/CHF is the clearest expression of the pair's fundamental identity as a bilateral safe-haven confrontation. The single most important bilateral dynamic is the dual safe-haven muting effect: when both the USD and CHF are safe-haven currencies, the standard VIX-driven pair directional trade breaks down, leaving fundamental factors to determine direction. At the tactical horizon, the fundamental factors tilt marginally bearish. The largest weighted contributor is Policy & Rates (+0.108) in favour of USD — the +363bps differential is real, the carry-to-vol ratio of +0.38 is the highest in the USD universe, and the SNB's zero rate floor means USD/CHF forward points are positively sloped. However, this mechanical advantage is overwhelmed when aggregated with the Inflation/Growth factor (-0.117): US stagflation (NFP -92K alongside core PCE 3.0%) creates a uniquely destructive combination for USD — the economy is too weak to support the currency but too inflationary for the Fed to relieve it. Switzerland's near-zero inflation (0.1% CPI) makes CHF the superior real-return currency despite offering zero nominal yield. The March 18–19 FOMC-SNB bilateral event cluster is the decisive near-term catalyst. A hawkish FOMC dot plot (zero 2026 cuts) followed by a SNB hold with muted intervention language would be the most pair-bullish outcome (+2–3% USD/CHF), roughly taking the pair toward 0.800–0.812. The base case — both hold, Fed dots show one cut, SNB delivers strong verbal intervention — produces a range-bound to modestly lower pair in the 0.770–0.785 zone. The Market Structure factor (-0.048 at adjusted weight 0.08) confirms the technical picture: USD/CHF is below all three declining MAs, with a bearish Descending Triangle target of 0.7396 and a bearish flag confirming downtrend continuation toward 0.765. Fiscal & Sovereign Risk (-0.028) adds background pressure as the US AA+ rating versus Switzerland's universal AAA activates in the current risk-off environment. Five of ten factors (1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 10) are either neutral or offsetting, which explains why tactical conviction remains at only 2/5 despite the directional lean. The carry advantage from Policy & Rates is real and mechanically supports USD/CHF at a floor, making it substantially harder to initiate aggressive short positions at current levels than the strategic case would suggest. Conviction is limited at 2/5 because the carry advantage is real and the March event cluster could produce a contrary outcome if either bank surprises.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: Among all USD pairs tactically, USD/CHF ranks as the 3rd most bearish USD expression (pair falls most), behind USD/JPY (BoJ hiking + PPP extreme) and EUR/USD (German fiscal stimulus, converging PMIs). Among CHF crosses, long CHF/USD (short USD/CHF) ranks 2nd most attractive CHF long, behind short EUR/CHF (where the 200bp ECB-SNB differential creates less carry drag). The unique bilateral characteristic: USD/CHF's carry drag is the highest in CHF's cross set (-363bps), making it the more expensive CHF long to carry but offering the highest directional payoff if the structural USD bear thesis plays out.

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The distribution is mildly right-skewed (EV positive) but the magnitude is insufficient to generate high conviction. Tactical positioning is not recommended for a directional trade at Conviction 2/5 — the near-term event cluster creates symmetric binary risk that limits the expected value of entering before the FOMC/SNB resolution.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.595 is decisively bearish. All 10 factors are bearish USD/CHF strategically. Score maps to Conviction 3/5 (between -0.50 and -0.70) — broad alignment but average confidence is moderate, reflecting genuine uncertainty around war duration and SNB intervention capacity. The pair is expected to trend materially lower over 1–6 months.
strategic.rationale: The strategic case for USD/CHF downside (CHF appreciation) is one of the broadest multi-factor bearish alignments possible: all 10 analytical factors point to pair downside. The dominant contributors are Policy & Rates (-0.144, weight 0.24) and External Balance (-0.112, weight 0.16). On Policy & Rates: the Fed is heading toward 3.00–3.25% by year-end 2026 (one to two cuts) while the SNB is pinned at 0.00%, meaning the differential will compress from +363bps toward +313–338bps — still large but directionally unfavourable for USD/CHF bulls, as the carry advantage narrows. On External Balance: the US-Swiss differential of 10.6pp of GDP (US -3.6% vs Swiss +7.0%) generates approximately CHF 30–35bn of structural CHF demand over 6 months, amplified by gold at $5,200+ generating unprecedented Swiss export revenues. The stagflationary US growth mix (Factor 2: -0.060) creates a self-reinforcing bearish loop — weak growth forces Fed cuts (rate headwind for USD), while sticky inflation prevents fiscal stimulus (growth remains weak), all flowing into a weaker USD. USD Liquidity (Factor 10: -0.072, weight 0.12) provides the mechanism: the Fed's QT-to-RMP pivot injects approximately $240B over 6 months, reducing the dollar scarcity premium and narrowing cross-currency bases globally. CHF's safe-haven premium over USD (Factor 3: -0.028) is reinforced by the erosion of USD's traditional safe-haven status — USD's negative NFA position (-$27.61T), political pressure on the Fed, and fiscal deterioration all undermine the USD's claim to safe-haven equality with CHF. Guidance & Credibility (-0.060, weight 0.12) shifts decisively bearish at the strategic horizon due to the Fed Chair transition from Powell to Warsh in May — creating an institutional guidance vacuum precisely when the Fed may be considering its first rate cut. The principal risk to this thesis is SNB intervention at the scale of 2020–2021 (CHF 50–100bn) combined with a war-driven USD safety bid that persists longer than the 6-week historical median for geopolitical safe-haven premiums. At Conviction 3/5, the strategic bearish view is high enough confidence to justify strategic short USD/CHF positions on rallies toward the 200-day SMA (0.7937), with a stop above 0.8123 (major technical resistance).
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: USD/CHF (short USD long CHF) ranks as the 2nd most attractive strategic CHF long expression, behind EUR/CHF (cheaper carry at -200bps vs -363bps, similar structural direction) and ahead of GBP/CHF (high carry drag at -375bps). As a strategic USD expression, short USD/CHF ranks 4th most attractive USD short, behind EUR/USD, USD/JPY (BoJ hiking), and GBP/USD — the higher carry cost of holding long CHF makes it less efficient than EUR or JPY as a USD short vehicle at the strategic horizon, though the structural CHF appreciation thesis is equally compelling and the external balance advantage for CHF over USD is the widest in G10 at 10.6pp of GDP.

base_case.path: Phase 1 (March–April): USD/CHF consolidates in 0.770–0.790 as the dual-safe-haven dynamic and March event cluster create volatility without sustained direction. Phase 2 (May–June): USD/CHF breaks below 0.770 as the Powell departure (May 15) creates institutional uncertainty and the Fed delivers or firmly prices its first cut, with the 0.7604 52-week low tested and potentially breached targeting the 0.7382 Fibonacci projection. Phase 3 (July–September): After potential war-resolution bounce of 2–4% (pair toward 0.790–0.810), USD/CHF settles in the 0.745–0.760 range as cumulative rate and external balance differentials assert.

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is phased across 1–6 months. Phase 1 (March–April): USD/CHF consolidates in 0.770–0.790 as the dual-safe-haven dynamic and March event cluster create volatility without sustained direction; the SNB meeting (March 19) produces a brief 50–80 pip bounce that fades within a week. Phase 2 (May–June): USD/CHF breaks below 0.770 as the Powell departure (May 15) creates institutional uncertainty and the Fed delivers or firmly prices its first cut; this phase sees the 0.7604 52-week low tested and potentially breached, targeting the 0.7382 Fibonacci projection. Phase 3 (July–September): If war resolves by June, an initial 2–4% CHF unwind (pair rally to 0.790–0.810) creates the best strategic entry point for re-shorting USD/CHF ahead of the structural USD decline continuation; by September, USD/CHF settles in the 0.745–0.760 range as cumulative rate and external balance differentials assert. Rallies above 0.8000 in any phase should be sold; 0.8123 is the absolute invalidation level for the strategic short.

risk_reward.expected_value: (50% × +276) + (25% × +647) + (25% × -553) = +138 + +161.75 - 138.25 = +161.5 pips positive EV. After carry cost (~130 pips over 6M), net directional alpha is approximately +31.5 pips.
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The probability-weighted EV is positive at +161.5 pips, but the carry drag (+363bps annualised = approximately +130 pips over 6M) reduces net alpha to approximately +31.5 pips. The distribution shape is modestly right-skewed (positive EV) but the asymmetry is not dramatic — the strategic trade works but requires patience to ride through SNB-intervention-induced bounces of 100–200 pips without stop-outs.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: UBS projects USD/CHF to stabilize around 0.78 over 2026. Aggregated consensus of major bank forecasts projects USD/CHF at approximately 0.781 in early 2026, declining to 0.772 by late 2026. More aggressive retail-focused forecasters project USD/CHF at 0.741 by September and 0.726 by October 2026.
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Bearish USD/CHF, Conviction 2/5 — pair trades 0.770–0.785 over 2–4 weeks, modest downside from current spot of 0.7797.
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Bearish USD/CHF, Conviction 3/5 — pair targets 0.745–0.760 by September 2026, approximately 15–30 pips below the conservative bank consensus.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Approximately 15–30 pips below conservative bank consensus (0.760–0.770) at 6M; significantly above aggressive retail forecasters (0.63–0.67 year-end).
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We disagree with UBS's 0.78 stabilisation forecast on three grounds. First, UBS's stabilisation forecast implicitly assumes SNB intervention is sufficient to cap CHF appreciation — historical evidence shows SNB intervention has slowed but never reversed sustained CHF appreciation driven by current account surpluses of Switzerland's magnitude. Second, gold at $5,200+ creates a CHF trade surplus amplifier that did not exist in prior cycles — each $100/oz gold increase adds approximately CHF 250–300M to monthly Swiss exports, a bilateral dynamic UBS appears not to fully embed. Third, the Fed's easing cycle compresses the carry advantage that is UBS's primary anchor — the market is already pricing 50bps of cuts that would narrow the differential from +363bps toward +313bps, removing the mechanical floor that UBS relies upon. We weigh the external balance factor (Factor 5) and reserve diversification flows (Factor 4 strategic) more heavily than sell-side consensus, which tends to anchor on carry-implied fair value.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: 0.7797 → 0.752 = +276 pips (3.5%) capturing alpha of 20–30 pips above the conservative bank consensus path of 0.770–0.772 at 6M.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If UBS is correct (0.78 stabilisation), the opportunity cost from a short position initiated at 0.7797 is minimal — carry (+approximately 130 pips over 6M) offsets most of the position loss. If the aggressive upside scenario (0.835) materialises, the loss is -553 pips.

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: The tactical and strategic stances are directionally aligned — both horizons point to USD/CHF downside (CHF appreciation). The difference is conviction magnitude and confidence: tactical is constrained by the dual-safe-haven muting effect and imminent event risk uncertainty (March 18–19 cluster), while strategic has the full weight of all 10 aligned factors. The cross-horizon consistency is the pair's strongest feature for positioning — there is no conflicting signal requiring a near-term reversal view before the structural trend reasserts. Post-March 19, tactical conviction will likely increase to 3/5 if the base case materialises, aligning fully with the strategic view.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: March 19, 2026 (SNB decision following FOMC) — the near-term resolution of key uncertainty after which tactical conviction is expected to increase from 2/5 to 3/5.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: The cross-horizon alignment supports initiating small strategic short USD/CHF positions on any bounce toward 0.790–0.800 (SNB-driven bounce post-March 19), building toward full position size if 0.7604 is breached. The tactical constraint (low conviction, balanced near-term risks) argues against initiating at current spot (0.7797), which sits between key support (0.7671) and key resistance (0.7804 50-day SMA) — not an ideal entry for either direction.

catalysts.rationale: The bilateral transmission is direct — a hot CPI print (+0.4%+ core MoM) reduces June cut probability, steepens the UST curve, and provides short-term USD/CHF upward pressure of 50–80 pips. A cool print (<0.2% core MoM) accelerates the June cut pricing and sends USD/CHF toward 0.770. Switzerland is unaffected by US CPI directly; the move is one-sided to the USD leg. The bilateral importance: this is the near-term binary that sets Fed expectations into the March 18 FOMC meeting, making it the most immediate catalyst in the window.

A BoJ hold with June hike signals narrows the CHF-JPY differential — the intra-safe-haven competition between CHF and JPY would shift marginally toward JPY, reducing demand for CHF relative to other safe havens. This has a small but observable impact on USD/CHF via reduced CHF bid pressure. The pair impact is indirect and secondary to the direct FOMC and SNB catalysts but provides context for CHF's safe-haven positioning relative to JPY, which is the other primary intra-safe-haven competitor.

The March dot plot is the single most important USD/CHF catalyst in the tactical window. Zero 2026 cuts (hawkish surprise): USD/CHF rallies to 0.800–0.815, potentially testing the 200-day SMA. One cut (neutral, consensus): USD/CHF range-bound, likely 0.778–0.785. Two or more cuts (dovish): USD/CHF drops toward 0.765–0.770. The bilateral transmission is through the rate differential channel, which is the largest positive factor for USD/CHF — any changes to the differential trajectory move the pair materially. The hold is priced at 95%+ but the dot plot distribution is wide and not priced.

The SNB's March 19 decision occurs one day after the FOMC, creating a sequential bilateral event where each leg is separately shocked. A SNB hold with strong verbal intervention language is the consensus — this would generate a brief 50–100 pip USD/CHF bounce as markets price intervention constraints on CHF appreciation. A surprise cut to -0.25% would generate 200–400 pip USD/CHF rally. Any softening of intervention rhetoric would push USD/CHF toward 0.765. The bilateral importance: this is the only quarterly SNB meeting window — the next is June 18, making this a high-frequency constraint on the strategic outlook.

This is a uniquely bilateral catalyst absent from most other USD pair analyses. The November 2025 framework reduced Swiss tariffs from 39% to 15% conditional on completing a legally binding agreement by March 31. Failure means tariff reversion to 15–39%, creating CHF-negative via reduced Swiss export competitiveness to its largest bilateral partner (US = 21.9% of Swiss exports). However, the bilateral trade disruption also reduces USD demand for Swiss goods, adding complexity. The White House is distracted by the Iran war, raising deal-failure risk above the market's current priced-in probability of near-zero. A failed deal is net bearish for the pair as the CHF export shock is more immediate than the USD trade flow effect.

catalysts.event: Powell Term Expiry / Warsh Fed Chair Transition
catalysts.rationale: The Fed Chair transition from Powell to Warsh creates a guidance vacuum precisely when the Fed is most likely to be considering its first rate cut. Markets will face a 4–8 week period where the new Chair's reaction function is unknown — Warsh's different communication style (more hawkish historically but operating in a weakening economy) creates uncertainty around whether the Fed will cut as expected or hold. This institutional uncertainty is USD-negative: it reduces the credibility of the Fed's signalling function relative to the SNB's clear and consistent communication framework. The SNB's three-member Governing Board is stable and has no equivalent transition risk.

Passage (polls: approximately 48% yes) triggers the guillotine clause — terminating all EU bilateral agreements including the newly signed Bilaterals III. For USD/CHF: initial Swiss capital repatriation from EU assets would be CHF-positive (pair falls), followed by structural CHF depreciation from lost EU market access (pair rises). The net 6-month impact for USD/CHF is uncertain in direction but certain in magnitude — the event would generate 3–5% pair volatility. Several analysts anticipate continued CHF appreciation near-term, but the referendum introduces a genuine binary that is materially underpriced in 6-month USD/CHF options.

Capital Economics projects a SNB cut at this meeting. If inflation fails to improve from 0.1%–0.3% by June and growth disappoints, cut probability rises meaningfully toward 30–40%. A SNB cut would trigger 200–400 pip USD/CHF rally — potentially the largest single-session pair move of 2026. A hold with continued intervention rhetoric maintains the structural CHF appreciation trend. This meeting is the most significant SNB catalyst in the strategic window because it coincides with the peak expected period of Fed cutting activity, making the bilateral rate path most uncertain at this juncture.

The 10% flat tariff (replacing IEEPA) expires. Congressional action is required to extend. No action means the tariff falls to zero (reducing Swiss export cost competitiveness advantage, mildly CHF-negative via reduced trade surplus); a higher tariff means compression of US demand for Swiss goods (more CHF-negative via trade flow disruption). The bilateral-specific nature of this catalyst — affecting USD/CHF directly through Swiss-US trade flows that constitute 21.9% of Swiss exports — makes it more material for this pair than for other USD crosses.

catalysts.event: FOMC September Decision + SEP (likely first Fed cut date)
catalysts.rationale: The first Fed cut confirms the easing trajectory, directly compressing the bilateral rate differential. Each 25bp Fed cut narrows the Fed-SNB differential from +363bps by 25bps, removing one of USD/CHF's structural supports. Two cuts (June + September) = 50bps compression = differential narrows to +313bps. The bilateral transmission is clear: a narrowing rate differential removes the mechanical carry anchor for USD/CHF and accelerates the structural CHF appreciation trend. This is priced at only approximately 47% probability, creating a potentially underpriced bearish catalyst.

catalysts.event: US Midterm Elections and Debt Ceiling Confrontation
catalysts.rationale: Historical pattern shows USD weakens into US midterm elections as fiscal policy uncertainty peaks. The debt ceiling confrontation coinciding with November 2026 elections amplifies the fiscal premium in USD risk. A Treasury auction failure or ceiling breach would be the most powerful USD/CHF bearish catalyst in the strategic window, potentially generating a 3–5% pair decline. This catalyst is the tail risk for USD/CHF bears — it has the largest potential pair impact of any event on the catalyst list but the lowest near-term certainty.

ECB hold at 2.00% maintains the 200bp ECB-SNB differential that structurally supports EUR/CHF above 0.90. For USD/CHF, the ECB's hold removes one potential shock to the CHF leg but does not drive the pair directly. The EUR/CHF stability (staying above 0.90) is a secondary positive for USD/CHF bulls as it confirms CHF appreciation is not accelerating against all crosses simultaneously. The ECB-SNB differential context provides a framework for understanding the SNB's own policy space — the SNB is unlikely to cut if the ECB is holding at 2.00%, as negative rates would widen the ECB-SNB spread to -225bps and risk disruptive EUR/CHF flows.

invalidators.condition: US core PCE rises above 3.5% YoY for two consecutive months
invalidators.mechanism: The Fed would be forced to pause or reverse its easing posture, widening the bilateral rate differential back toward its maximum in USD's favour. A re-acceleration of US inflation would also reduce the stagflationary narrative — the US economy would shift from stagflation (bad for USD) to pure inflation (supports USD via higher-for-longer rates). The bilateral rate channel would reassert as the dominant factor, potentially pushing USD/CHF above 0.80.

invalidators.condition: USD/CHF closes above the 200-day SMA (0.7937) for 5 consecutive sessions
invalidators.mechanism: Systematic CTA flows would flip from short to long as the 200-day SMA is the primary technical trigger for the approximately $2–4B of estimated CTA short USD/CHF positions. A sustained break above 0.7937 would trigger mechanical covering and potentially flip the CTA signal to long, creating a self-reinforcing rally toward 0.810–0.820 as stops above 0.7937 are triggered sequentially.

invalidators.condition: SNB cuts to -0.25% at any quarterly meeting
invalidators.mechanism: Negative rates historically create an immediate CHF-negative shock as carry dynamics shift from paying zero to hold CHF to paying 25bps to hold CHF, triggering unwinding of leveraged CHF longs and re-engagement of asset manager carry shorts. The bilateral rate differential would narrow from +363bps to +388bps (paradoxically widening in USD's favour), reversing the differential compression thesis that underpins the strategic bearish view.

invalidators.condition: Iran war fully escalates with Hormuz closed for 2+ weeks sustained above $120 WTI
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral (dual safe-haven pair bounces both ways)
invalidators.mechanism: The USD's reserve currency demand would match CHF's safe-haven bid precisely at extreme crisis intensity, creating a range-bound pair at 0.790–0.820 while other safe-haven expression (gold, Treasuries) dominates. US energy exporter status would provide a unique terms of trade benefit versus Switzerland's import dependency, partially reversing the current CHF safe-haven advantage and supporting USD/CHF above 0.79.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: WTI crude price sustained above $120 AND confirmed Hormuz shipping disruption data

invalidators.condition: VIX falls sustainably below 16 for two consecutive weeks following conflict resolution
invalidators.mechanism: The CHF safe-haven premium would collapse faster than the USD structural weakness can reassert. Historical precedent (post-COVID, post-Gulf War I) shows CHF safe-haven unwinds of 3–5% within 4–8 weeks of geopolitical resolution, driven by the asset manager CHF short (-54,527 contracts) rebuilding toward pre-war levels of -70K+ contracts. This mechanical CHF selling would generate a 2–4% USD/CHF recovery toward 0.800–0.815 before structural factors reassert.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: VIX daily close and weekly average

invalidators.condition: June 14 Swiss referendum passes triggering EU bilateral agreement termination
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral (pair ambiguous — opposing forces of uncertain relative magnitude)
invalidators.mechanism: The initial CHF-positive capital repatriation flow (Swiss investors selling EU assets back into CHF) and the subsequent CHF-negative structural trade shock (lost EU market access representing approximately 43% of Swiss exports) create opposing forces over 6 months. The net USD/CHF impact is uncertain in direction but certain in magnitude — 3–5% pair volatility is expected. The thesis is invalidated because the analytical framework cannot confidently assign a directional pair impact given the competing flows.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Swiss referendum polls (weekly) and vote outcome on June 14

signposts.confirms_if: Remains above spot (USD/CHF below 200-day); slope continues downward
signposts.invalidates_if: USD/CHF closes above 0.7937 for 5 or more consecutive sessions
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the single most important technical level for USD/CHF — CTA systematic strategies are entirely positioned short below this level. A sustained break above would trigger mechanical covering of an estimated $2–4B of short USD/CHF positions, generating a self-reinforcing rally. For no other CHF cross does the 200-day SMA carry this magnitude of positional significance.

signposts.confirms_if: Remains below CHF 460bn in a 4-week window (limited intervention — CHF appreciation resuming)
signposts.invalidates_if: Rises above CHF 480bn in a 4-week window (large-scale intervention at 2020–2021 comparable scale moderating CHF appreciation)
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the most direct observable proxy for SNB FX intervention — a capability unique to the CHF leg of this pair with no USD equivalent. For USD/CHF specifically, rapidly rising sight deposits signal SNB selling CHF (pair support). The CHF 30bn/month threshold is comparable to 2020–2021 intervention scale, which historically slowed but never reversed structural CHF appreciation cycles driven by current account surpluses.

signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above $4,800 (Swiss gold trade surplus remains amplified, CHF structurally bid via export revenues)
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below $4,500 (gold-CHF safe-haven correlation breaks; Swiss monthly trade surplus normalises removing the novel CHF tailwind)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Gold at $5,200+ is generating unprecedented Swiss gold export revenues — Switzerland processes approximately 25% of world gold, making this a uniquely bilateral USD/CHF variable absent from other USD pair analyses. The gold-Swiss trade surplus linkage is the most novel element of the current CHF appreciation cycle and represents the primary underpriced CHF positive in institutional models.

signposts.indicator: USD-CHF Rate Differential (Fed Funds vs SARON)
signposts.confirms_if: Narrows below +330bps (Fed cut delivered or firmly priced; differential compressing — removing the primary USD/CHF structural support)
signposts.invalidates_if: Widens above +390bps (Fed stays on hold while SNB cuts to -0.25% — differential expands in USD's favour, reversing the compression thesis)
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the single most important fundamental anchor for USD/CHF — the +363bps carry differential is the pair's sole structural support in the face of 10 bearish factors. Monitoring the trajectory of this differential is essential because it is the mechanism through which the strategic bearish thesis plays out. No other CHF cross has a rate differential of comparable magnitude, making USD/CHF uniquely sensitive to changes in the Fed's rate path.
signposts.frequency: Each central bank meeting; OIS curve monitored daily

signposts.indicator: CFTC CHF Leveraged Fund Net Futures Position
signposts.confirms_if: Builds toward +15,000–20,000 contracts (CHF longs building but not at contrarian extreme — momentum intact and safe-haven demand persisting)
signposts.invalidates_if: Flips to net short CHF below -10,000 contracts (structural carry sellers dominate; safe-haven premium fully unwound — triggering CHF-negative momentum)
signposts.bilateral_importance: For USD/CHF specifically, the CHF leveraged fund position is the cleanest leading indicator of institutional safe-haven demand — the CHF leg's positioning is directly tradeable as a USD/CHF directional signal in a way that USD aggregate positioning is not. A rapid flip from long to short would signal war de-escalation and safe-haven premium collapse: the single most powerful catalyst for a USD/CHF multi-percent rally and the primary contrarian risk to the bearish thesis.

signposts.confirms_if: Remains negative or below +50K for 2 additional months (labour market deterioration forces Fed cuts, compressing bilateral rate differential)
signposts.invalidates_if: Rebounds to +150K or more for 2 consecutive months (labour market recovery removes Fed easing pressure, stabilising the +363bps differential)
signposts.bilateral_importance: USD/CHF is uniquely sensitive to US labour market data because the bilateral rate differential is the pair's primary structural support — deteriorating US labour market is the catalyst that forces the Fed's hand and narrows the +363bps advantage. No other G10 pair has the same mechanical sensitivity: for EUR/USD, EUR growth also matters; for USD/JPY, BoJ hike prospects matter. For USD/CHF, the SNB's zero rate floor means only the Fed side of the differential can move, making US employment data the primary rate-channel catalyst.

assumptions: USD/CHF spot rate referenced at 0.7797 (March 11, 2026 morning). Minor intraday variation noted.

The -363bps bilateral carry differential is calculated using the effective Fed Funds rate (3.625%) vs SNB policy rate (0.00%); the SARON at -0.07% provides a marginally more negative CHF funding cost than the policy rate.

Forward points for USD/CHF are estimated from the interest rate differential using covered interest parity (CIP). Exact forward rates require live market data; Bloomberg terminal verification recommended before execution.
The USD/CHF cross-currency basis is estimated directionally at approximately -10 to -20bps (CHF at a modest premium). Exact figures require Bloomberg XCCY screen or equivalent.

USD/CHF implied volatility (9.5% 1M, 8.5% 3M) and risk reversal direction (CHF calls bid) are sourced from the CHF dossier and cross-referenced with the current market structure analysis. Exact IV percentile requires options market data.
Technical levels (SMAs at 0.7804/0.7870/0.7937) are cross-referenced across multiple technical sources and the CHF dossier; the 0.7382 Fibonacci projection is the ActionForex consensus target.
Positioning data (CFTC) reflects March 3, 2026 (released March 7). One-week lag means post-war-onset positioning shift toward CHF is partially embedded but not fully captured.

The dual-safe-haven Factor 3 weight reduction (-0.03 from 0.10 to 0.07 tactical) is a judgment-based adjustment reflecting the unique bilateral nature of USD/CHF as a same-safe-haven-category pair. This reduces the tactical contribution of risk sentiment, which has historically produced muted USD/CHF moves versus other CHF crosses.
PPP fair value of USD/CHF at approximately 1.00 is derived from the USD dossier (-33% CHF deviation); this is a slow-moving structural reference, not a tactical signal.
SNB intervention scale is estimated from sight deposit data (+CHF 4.4bn first post-war week); exact intervention amounts are not disclosed by the SNB.

The 52-week range high of approximately 0.9000 reflects the pair trading above 0.90 in early 2025; the 52-week percentile calculation of approximately 14th percentile uses the 0.7604–0.9000 range with current spot at 0.7797.

The June 14 referendum passage probability of 33–35% is an analytical estimate based on CHF dossier commentary indicating polls at approximately 48% in favour; formal polling averages should be consulted for precise probabilities.
